full_response:
We start from the ordinary Schwarzschild line–element written in Schwarzschild coordinates ( t , r , θ , φ )

ds² = −(1 − 2GM / r ) dt² + dr² /(1 − 2GM / r ) + r² (dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).  (1)

Here the mass parameter M is assumed, as usual, to be positive and the radial coordinate is restricted to r ≥ 0.  
The problem asks us to imagine a “fifth region’’ in which we formally allow r to take negative values, r < 0, while keeping M > 0, and to show that this is equivalent to keeping r > 0 but letting the mass be negative.

Step 1.  Introduce a strictly positive radial coordinate.  
For r < 0 define

ρ ≡ −r   ⇒  r = −ρ (ρ > 0),   dr = −dρ ⇒ dr² = dρ².  (2)

Step 2.  Rewrite every factor of the metric in terms of ρ.

The prefactor in g_tt :

1 − 2GM / r = 1 − 2GM /(−ρ) = 1 + 2GM / ρ.  (3)

The radial part:

dr² /(1 − 2GM / r ) = dρ² /(1 + 2GM / ρ).  (4)

The angular part:

r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²) = ρ²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).  (5)

Hence (1) becomes

ds² = −(1 + 2GM / ρ) dt² + dρ² /(1 + 2GM / ρ) + ρ² (dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).  (6)

Step 3.  Recognise (6) as a Schwarzschild line–element with a negative mass.  
Introduce a new mass parameter M̃ defined by

M̃ ≡ −M.  (7)

Then for any positive ρ

1 − 2G M̃/ρ = 1 − 2G(−M)/ρ = 1 + 2GM/ρ,  (8)

and (6) can be rewritten as

ds² = −(1 − 2G M̃ / ρ) dt² + dρ² /(1 − 2G M̃ / ρ) + ρ² (dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).  (9)

Equation (9) is exactly the Schwarzschild metric with the usual (positive) radial coordinate ρ and with mass parameter M̃ = −M < 0.

Therefore

• Allowing r < 0 while keeping M > 0 is mathematically identical to  
• Keeping r > 0 but replacing the mass by a negative value M → −M.

Nothing physically new is obtained: the “fifth region’’ is just an ordinary Schwarzschild spacetime with a negative mass, separated from the usual regions by the curvature singularity at r = 0 so that no world-line can pass from one to the other.

\boxed{\text{Region }(r<0,\;M>0)\; \Longleftrightarrow \; (r>0,\;M_{\text{eff}}=-M)<0} 

Confidence: 86%
